title: Yorkshire Puddings
subtitle: The proper tall, crisp version with smoking-hot fat
excerpt: A Yorkshire pudding rises because the batter hits smoking-hot fat in a very hot oven. The fat must be in the tin and in the oven before the batter goes in. Get that right and the rest follows. The batter is equal parts egg, milk, and flour by volume; resting it for at least 30 minutes makes the difference between a pudding that rises and one that does not.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: original Yorkshire pudding method. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: batter pudding under the roast. Heston Blumenthal, Heston Blumenthal at Home (2011): the resting and equal-parts formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked Yorkshire puddings freeze well. Freeze on a tray, then bag. Reheat from frozen at 200°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batter and use two tins for a larger batch. Batter keeps refrigerated for up to 24 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter rests for a minimum of 30 minutes and can rest overnight in the fridge. The cold batter makes no difference to the rise if the fat is hot enough.
recipe.temperatureNote: 210°C fan; the oven must be screaming hot

glossaryTerms.definition: Allowing a flour-egg-milk batter to rest for at least 30 minutes after mixing, so the gluten relaxes, the starch fully hydrates, and the puddings rise more uniformly when they meet the hot fat.

content.text: Yorkshire puddings rise because steam inside the batter expands rapidly when it meets very hot fat. If the fat is not smoking when the batter goes in, the puddings will be flat and dense. There is no salvaging a tepid tin.
The batter formula is simple: 140 g of plain flour, 4 eggs, and 200 ml of whole milk.

for at least 30 minutes is not optional: the rest allows the gluten to relax and the flour to absorb the liquid fully. This makes the batter more fluid, which is what allows it to rise rather than set before the steam can lift it.

items.prepNote: or vegetable oil; must be a fat with a high smoke point

content.text: A 12-hole Yorkshire pudding tin with cups approximately 7 cm in diameter. Shallower tins produce flatter puddings; the taller the cup, the more the pudding rises above the rim. Beef dripping gives the best flavour; vegetable oil works just as well for rise.

Sift {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and crack in all 4 {{eggs}} eggs. Beat with a balloon whisk, drawing in flour from the sides. Gradually pour in {{whole-milk}} of whole milk, whisking continuously until the batter is smooth and the consistency of double cream. There should be no lumps. Leave to rest for at least 30 minutes at room temperature, or up to 24 hours in the fridge.

Heat the oven to 230°C (210°C fan). Put roughly a teaspoon of {{beef-dripping}} of dripping into each cup of the Yorkshire pudding tin. Slide the tin into the oven for 10 minutes. The fat must be shimmering and beginning to smoke. Do not open the oven during the preheat.

Working quickly, pour the batter into a jug (if it is in a bowl). Pull the tin from the oven, close the door immediately, and pour batter into each cup to about two-thirds full. The batter should sizzle loudly as it hits the fat. Return the tin to the oven immediately and bake for 20 to 25 minutes without opening the door. The puddings are done when they are deep golden brown and risen well above the rim. Opening the door before 20 minutes will make them collapse.

rows.cause: Fat not hot enough, oven not hot enough, or door opened during baking
rows.fix: Preheat the tin in the oven for the full 10 minutes. Ensure the oven is at 230°C. Do not open the door for the first 20 minutes.

rows.symptom: Puddings crisp at the edges but doughy in the centre
rows.cause: Too much batter per cup, or oven ran low
rows.fix: Fill cups only two-thirds full. Check oven temperature with a thermometer.

rows.cause: Insufficient fat in each cup, or non-stick tin worn
rows.fix: Use at least a heaped teaspoon of fat per cup and ensure the fat is shimmering before pouring batter.

rows.symptom: Puddings collapse after leaving the oven
rows.cause: Taken out before fully set, or the gluten structure is underdeveloped
rows.fix: Bake until the sides are deeply set and rigid. A slight softness in the centre is fine; a wobble in the walls is not.

content.text: The Yorkshire pudding originated as a batter cooked beneath a joint on a spit, catching the dripping fat. Eliza Acton's 1845 recipe is one of the earliest printed versions. Mrs Beeton's 1861 edition includes it as standard Sunday practice. The modern convention of serving small individual puddings came later; the original was a large flat pudding served as a first course before the meat, to fill guests up on the cheaper batter and reduce their appetite for the more expensive beef.
